title: FYS: Right and Wrong
description: Reading and discussion of classic and contemporary philosophical texts on morality: its nature, status, and importance.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Open only to newly admitted students in U0 or U1 who may take only one FYS

title: FYS: Knowledge and Ideas in Early Modern Philosophy
description: An introduction to central issues in the philosophy of the early modern period through an examination of works by, for example, Descartes, Malebranche, Spinoza, Locke, Leibniz, Berkeley and Hume.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Open only to newly admitted students in U0 or U1 who may take only one FYS

title: FYS: Minds, Brain, and Machines
description: An introduction to the philosophical foundations of the sciences of the mind.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Open only to newly admitted students in U0 or U1 who may take only one FYS

title: Introduction to Philosophy 1
description: A course treating some of the central problems of philosophy: the mind-body problem, freedom, scepticism and certainty, fate, time, and the existence of God.

title: Introduction to Philosophy 2
description: An introduction to some of the major problems of philosophy. This course does not duplicatePHIL 200.

title: The Good Life
description: Explores how philosophers from Socrates to Sartre wrestle with the question of how we should live, including ancient philosophers who present their teachings as pathways to happiness, virtue, and peace of mind; medieval philosophers who seek to understand God’s will; and modern philosophers who grapple with the tension between community and freedom. Analyzes debates of fundamental moral and existential questions: What things really matter? Does it pay to be good? What constitutes the best society? Can we escape the roller coaster of our emotions? Examines how answers are intertwined with beliefs about God, nature, knowledge, the soul, and politics.

title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence Ethics
description: An introduction to several important debates in the ethics of artificial intelligence (AI), including: artificial general intelligence, artificial consciousness, existential risk, and the moral status of advanced AI systems, to practical ethical and political issues raised by the uses of AI systems, including opacity, discrimination, privacy,disinformation and impacts on work and wealth distribution.

title: Introduction to Deductive Logic 1
description: An introduction to propositional and predicate logic; formalization of arguments, truth tables, systems of deduction, elementary metaresults, and related topics.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Not open to students who are taking or have taken MATH 318

title: Introduction to History and Philosophy of Science 2
description: A survey of the development of modern science since the Eighteenth Century.

title: Introduction to Moral Philosophy 1
description: A survey of a number of historically important and influential theories. Philosophers to be discussed may include Aristotle, Hume, Kant, Bentham, Mill, and Moore.

title: Contemporary Moral Issues
description: An introductory discussion of central ethical questions (the value of persons, or the relationship of rights and utilities, for example) through the investigation of currently disputed social and political issues. Specific issues to be discussed may include pornography and censorship, affirmative action, civil disobedience, punishment, abortion, and euthanasia.

title: Political Philosophy 1
description: An introduction to contemporary philosophy of politics by concentrating on a number of contested concepts, such as freedom, justice and equality, in contemporary political philosophy and practice.

title: Introduction to Feminist Theory
description: An introduction to feminist theory as political theory. Emphasis is placed on the plurality of analyses and proposals that constitute contemporary feminist thought. Some of the following are considered: liberal feminism, marxist and socialist feminism, radical feminism, postmodern feminism, francophone feminism, and the contributions to feminist theory by women of colour and lesbians.
notes_raw: Note: Since this course is being taught abroad, the Victoria Day statutory holiday will not be taken into consideration. Therefore, students are expected to attend their lecture on Monday, May 19, 2014.

title: Philosophical Fundamentals
description: An intensive study of basic philosophical skills; reading, writing, analysis, and argumentation.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisites: two previous courses in philosophy, one of which must be PHIL 210 or written consent of the Department
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Open only to and required of Philosophy Honours and Joint Honours students
name_variants: take a special 3-credit course ( PHIL 301 Philosophical Fundamentals

title: Philosophy of Mind
description: A survey of major positions of the mind-body problem, focusing on such questions as: Do we have minds and bodies? Can minds affect bodies? Is mind identical to body? If so, in what sense "identical"? Can physical bodies be conscious.

title: Intermediate Logic
description: A second course in Logic. NB. The course will be technical in nature, and some mathematical aptitude is essential. The emphasis is on the expressive properties of standard logical systems, including implications for the philosophy of mathematics. We will study the Completeness of First-Order Logic, then the 'limitative' theorems of Tarski and Gödel.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 210 or equivalent

title: Philosophy of Mathematics
description: This course provides an historically informed introduction to philosophy of mathematics. It gives the student an overview of prominent issues and arguments, to enable her to follow and discuss contemporary research in philosophy of mathematics.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 210

title: Philosophy of Race
description: An introduction to issues in the philosophy of race, for example: the metaphysical status of race; the biology of racial categories; the social construction of race; the relationship between race and racism; the phenomenology of racialized subjectivity; or, intersections of race, gender, and other identity categories.

title: Ethical Theory
description: A course focusing on central questions in ethical theory such as the nature of the good and the right and the factors which determine moral rightness and wrongness.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: one of PHIL 230, PHIL 237, PHIL 242, PHIL 343, or written permission of the instructor

title: Aesthetics
description: An introduction to issues central to aesthetic theory; the nature of aesthetic judgment, perception of the aesthetic object, the nature of the art object.

title: Philosophy of Science 1
description: A discussion of philosophical problems as they arise in the context of scientific practice and enquiry. Such issues as the philosophical presuppositions of the physical and social sciences, the nature of scientific method and its epistemological implications will be addressed.

title: Biomedical Ethics
description: An investigation of ethical issues as they arise in the practice of medicine (informed consent, e.g.) or in the application of medical technology (in vitro fertilization, euthanasia, e.g.)

title: Medieval and Renaissance Political Theory
description: A study of Medieval and Renaissance political theory.

title: Greek Political Theory
description: An examination of the ethical and political theories of ancient Greece, especially those of Plato and Aristotle.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Not open to students who have taken POLI 333

title: Philosophy of Law 1
description: A discussion of the nature of justice and law, and of the relationship between them.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: This course is intended for students with a non-professional interest in law, as well as for those considering law as a profession

title: Environmental Philosophy
description: An introduction to environmental ethics; potentially also such related fields as philosophy of ecology, environmental aesthetics, and/or other philosophical topics demonstrably relevant to environmental issues.

title: History and Philosophy of Ancient Science
description: Topics in ancient pure mathematics (geometry and number theory), "mixed mathematics" (astronomy, music theory, optics, mechanics), and/or natural science (including medicine), studied with a view to philosophical issues raised by the content of ancient science and/or by the logic of scientific argument.

title: The Presocratic Philosophers
description: An examination of the surviving fragments of the presocratic philosophers and schools of philosophy, as well as later reports of their views.

title: Plato
description: An examination of some of the philosophical problems (those in logic, epistemology, metaphysics, and ethics, e.g.) found in a selection of Plato's dialogues.

title: Aristotle
description: An examination of selected works by Aristotle. The course considers issues in moral philosophy as well as those found in the logical treatises, the Physics and Metaphysics, and in the philosophy of mind.

title: Early Medieval Philosophy
description: An examination of selected works in the Christian, Islamic and Jewish traditions. Topics in moral and political philosophy, logic and metaphysics, philosophical psychology and epistemology, philosophy of science, and philosophical theology may be discussed.

title: 17th Century Philosophy
description: An examination of the work of such seventeenth-century philosophers as Descartes, Hobbes, Gassendi, Malebranche, Leibniz, and the Cambridge Platonists.

title: 18th Century Philosophy
description: A survey of eighteenth century philosophy, especially British philosophy. Attention is given to fundamental metaphysical, epistemological, and moral issues as reflected in the work of such philosophers as Locke, Shaftesbury, Berkeley, Hutcheson, Butler, Hume and Reid.

title: 18th and Early 19th Century German Philosophy
description: An examination of the works of such philosophers as Kant, Fichte, Jacobi, Schelling, and Hegel.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 360 or PHIL 361 is recommended

title: 19th Century Philosophy
description: An examination of the works of such 19th century philosophers as Mach, Helmholtz, Dedekind, Frege, Marx, Kierkegaard, Schopenhauer, Nietzsche, Mill and Bradley.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: A previous course in philosophy is recommended

title: Existentialism
description: This course will examine the nature of existentialist thought as represented in various philosophical and literary texts. Particular themes to be examined include freedom, alienation, responsibility and choice, and the nature of self.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: one course in philosophy

title: Tutorial 01
description: Individualized guided research on an approved topic in philosophy.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Open to second year Full Honours students in Philosophy and to other students, with consent of the Department

title: Tutorial 02
description: Individualized guided research on an approved topic in philosophy.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Open to second year Full Honours students in Philosophy and to other students, with consent of the Department

title: Topics in Philosophy of Logic and Mathematics
description: A course focusing on some philosophical issue (e.g., the nature of numbers or the relation of truth to provability) as it arises in the study of mathematics and logic.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisites: PHIL 210 or the equivalent, and one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Philosophy of Language
description: An examination of central notions in the philosophy of language (reference, meaning, and truth, e.g.), the puzzles these notions give rise to, and the relevance of these notions to such questions as: What is language? How is communication possible? What is understanding? Is language rule-governed.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisites: PHIL 210 or equivalent and one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Epistemology
description: A discussion of central topics in the theory of knowledge. The questions addressed in the course may include: What is knowledge? Do we have any knowledge? What is the relation between knowledge and belief? When is belief justified? Is all knowledge conscious knowledge.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 210 or equivalent and one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Metaphysics
description: An examination of central questions in metaphysics in their historical and contemporary forms. Topics may be chosen from such issues as: personal identity, the nature of space and time, the nature of events and properties, possible worlds, and the problem of realism.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisites: PHIL 210 or equivalent and one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Topics in Critical Philosophy of Race
description: An advanced discussion of topics in the critical philosophy of race.

title: Metaethics
description: A study of the status of sentences containing moral terms, judgements about moral claims, and the nature of moral facts.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 334 or written permission of the instructor

title: Aesthetics 2
description: An advanced discussion of issues in aesthetics.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 336 or written permission of the instructor

title: Philosophy of Science 2
description: An analysis of some key philosophical ideas in science and technology, e.g. problem, explanation, forecast, testability and truth.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 341 or written permission of the instructor

title: Topics in Feminist Theory
description: Advanced discussion of topical and central themes in feminist theory.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 242 and one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Early Modern Political Theory
description: A survey of political and moral theory from the Reformation to the French Revolution including Luther, Montaigne, Descartes, Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau and Smith.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: at least one course in political philosophy

title: 19th Century Political Theory
description: An examination of various strands of political theory since Rousseau, concentrating on such themes as the understanding of modernity and theories of liberal society.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: at least one course in political philosophy
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Not open to students who have taken POLI 434

title: Current Issues in Political Philosophy
description: Selected issues in contemporary political philosophy.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: at least one course in political philosophy

title: Major Philosophers 1
description: This seminar will give detailed attention to the work of one philosopher or to a single philosophical theme addressed by several philosophers. Emphasis will be placed on understanding how the metaphysical, epistemological, and moral views of a figure or figures are internally related. Topic will vary from year to year.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Later Greek Philosophy
description: An examination of some of the major post-Aristotelian schools of philosophy. Texts from the Peripatetic, Stoic, Epicurean, Sceptical, Platonic, and medical traditions may be considered. Problems in logic, ethics, physics, epistemology, and metaphysics will be addressed.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 354 or PHIL 355

title: Ancient Metaphysics and Natural Philosophy
description: An examination of central themes of ancient metaphysics and/or natural philosophy as treated by two or more contrasting philosophers or philosophical traditions - probably including Plato and/or Aristotle, and possibly including some Hellenistic or post-Hellenistic schools.

title: Ancient Moral Theory
description: An examination of central themes of ancient moral theory as treated by two or more contrasting philosophers or philosophical traditions - probably including Plato and/or Aristotle, and possibly some Hellenistic or post-Hellenistic schools.

title: Major Philosophers 2
description: This seminar will give detailed attention to the work of one philosopher or to a single philosophical theme addressed by several philosophers. Emphasis will be placed on understanding how the metaphysical, epistemological, and moral views of a figure or figures are internally related.

title: Topics in Contemporary Analytic Philosophy
description: An advanced discussion of major themes in the analytic tradition.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 370, PHIL 415 or written permission of instructor

title: Phenomenology
description: A study of phenomenology from a historical and thematic perspective. The course will typically involve the study of central thinkers such as Husserl, Heidegger, or Merleau-Ponty, with an examination of the nature and development of the phenomenological movement.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Topics in Contemporary European Philosophy
description: Advanced discussion of selected themes in contemporary European philosophy.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Topics in the History of Philosophy
description: An advanced discussion of some theme and/or problem in the history of philosophy.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: one intermediate course in philosophy

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: Topics in philosophy.  Topic varies by year.

title: Tutorial 04
description: Open to third year Full Honours students in Philosophy, and to other students, with consent of the Department.

title: Tutorial 05
description: Open to third year Joint Honours students in Philosophy, and to other students, with consent of the Department.

title: Tutorial 06
description: Open to third year Full Honours students in Philosophy, and to other students, with consent of the Department.

title: Tutorial 07
description: Individualized guided research on an approved topic in philosophy.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Open only to students in the Philosophy Honours and Joint Honours programmes.

title: Seminar: Philosophy of Mind
description: An advanced course devoted to specific topics in the philosophy of mind.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 506.

title: Pro-Seminar 1
description: A series of seminars on selected topics designed for professional training to graduate students. Topics will be selected from the general area of Value Theory.

title: Seminar on Advanced Logic 2
description: Seminar on a particular topic in advanced logic.  Topic varies by year.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor .
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 510.

title: Seminar: Philosophy of Logic and Mathematics
description: Seminar on a particular topic in philosophy of logic and mathematics.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 511.

title: Seminar: Philosophy of Language
description: An advanced course devoted to a topic in the philosophy of language.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 515.

title: Seminar: Epistemology
description: An advanced course devoted to a topic in the theory of knowledge.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 519.

title: Seminar: Metaphysics
description: An advanced course devoted to a topic in metaphysics.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 521.

title: Seminar: History and Philosophy of Science
description: An advanced course devoted to a topic on the history and philosophy of science.

title: Seminar: Critical Philosophy of Race
description: An advanced course examining a question, theme, or thinker in the critical philosophy of race.

title: Seminar: Ethics
description: Seminar on a particular topic in ethics.  Topic varies by year.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 534.

title: Seminar: History of Ethics
description: An advanced course devoted to a topic on the history of ethics.

title: Seminar: Aesthetics
description: An advanced course devoted to a specific topic in the area of aesthetics and/or the philosophy of art.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 536.

title: Seminar: Philosophy of Science
description: An advanced course devoted to a topic in the philosophy of science.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 541.

title: Seminar: Feminist Theory
description: An advanced course devoted to a specific topic in feminist theory: e.g., a major figure; or theme, such as sex/gender, embodiment, race, subjectivity, agency, representation, politics, nature/culture, discourse and power; or a feminist approach to the history of philosophy, ethics, social/political philosophy, epistemology, philosophy of science, phenomenology, or metaphysics.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Any 400-level, 3-credit course in political philosophy, or permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 542.

title: Seminar: Medical Ethics
description: An advanced course devoted to a particular philosophical problem as it arises in the context of medical practice or the application of medical technology.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 543.

title: Political Theory
description: Seminar on a particular topic in political theory.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 544.

title: Seminar: Philosophy of Law
description: An advanced course devoted to a particular topic in the philosophy of law. Subject varies from year to year.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 548.

title: Seminar: Ancient Philosophy 1
description: Seminar on a particular topic in ancient philosophy. Topic varies by year.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 550.

title: Seminar: Ancient Philosophy 2
description: An advanced course on a philosopher or philosophical issue articulated in antiquity.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): at least one course in ancient philosophy and the specific requirements of individual instructors.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 551.

title: Medieval Philosophy
description: An advanced course devoted to a particular topic in medieval philosophy. Subject varies from year to year.

title: Seminar: 17th Century Philosophy
description: An advanced course on a seventeenth-century philosopher or philosophical issue.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): PHIL 360 or written permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 560.

title: Seminar: 18th Century Philosophy
description: An advanced course on an eighteenth-century philosopher or philosophical issue.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 561.

title: Seminar: Contemporary Analytic Philosophy
description: An advanced course on some major analytic philosopher, or some issue of central importance in the analytic tradition.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 570.

title: Seminar: Contemporary European Philosophy
description: An advanced course on contemporary European philosophy or some important issue in the Continental tradition.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 575.

title: Seminar: Problems of Philosophy 1
description: Seminar on a particular topic in philosophy. Topic varies by year.
restrictions_raw: Restriction(s): Not open to students who have taken PHIL 580.

title: Pro-Seminar 3
description: A series of seminars on selected topics designed to provide professional training to graduate students. Topics will be selected from the general area of Metaphysics/Epistemology.

title: Fundamentals of Logic
description: A course in intermediate logic for graduate students in Philosophy, covering such topics as axiomatic systems, formal semantics, consistency, completeness, the limitative results, intuitionistic logic, formal theories of truth, aspects of the development of logic.

title: Candidacy Examination
description: Preparation and submission of a candidacy research paper that must be passed by all doctoral candidates in order to continue in the doctoral program.
restrictions_raw: Restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 690.

title: Teaching Reflection and Practice
description:
Course design, development, delivery, and evaluation of a course that the student has been assigned to teach. Aspects of course design and in-class student
evaluations will be provided by a Faculty mentor.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite: PHIL 702
restrictions_raw: Restriction: Graduate students in philosophy

title: Dissertation Seminar
description: Dissertation planning and seminar.
prerequisites_raw: Prerequisites: PHIL 701
restrictions_raw: Restrictions: Open only to students enrolled in a Ph.D. program in the Department of Philosophy.

title: Guided Research in Ethics 1
description: Guided research on a theme and/or problem in ethics.

title: Guided Research in Ethics 2

description: Guided research on a theme and/or problem in the history of philosophy.

title: Guided Research: History of Philosophy 2
description: Guided research on a theme and/or problem in the histoory of philosophy.

description: Guided research on a theme and/or problem in the philosophy of politics.

title: Guided Research: Philosophy of Politics 2
